conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official guidance to ensure I can provide you with accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Do you have any children under the age of 16, or children aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I've got three children actually. There's Alex who's 3, Blake who's 8, and Charlie who's 10.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you for that information! Now I need to understand a bit more about your situation with Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do – I live in Leeds.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Now I need to understand your living arrangements with your children.

Do Alex, Blake, and Charlie all live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex and Charlie live at home with me, but Blake doesn't – he's actually been in hospital for a few months now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand, thank you for sharing that. I'm sorry to hear Blake has been in hospital.

How long has Blake been in hospital? Has it been more than 12 weeks?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's been a while now – I think it's been about 18 weeks or so.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, there are some special rules that apply. 

Do you regularly spend money on Blake while he's in hospital - for example, on things like clothes, toiletries, toys, or other items for him?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do – I still buy him things like clothes, magazines, snacks he likes, that sort of thing. I want to make sure he's comfortable while he's in there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's very thoughtful of you. Thank you for that information.

Just to confirm - is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for any of your three children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. I have just a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

Are you responsible for Alex, Blake, and Charlie - meaning you're either living with them or contributing at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm responsible for all of them. Alex and Charlie live with me at home, and for Blake, even though he's in hospital, I'm still spending money on him regularly to make sure he has what he needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One final question:

Are any of your children currently in care (looked after by the local council), or is the local council paying towards any of their accommodation or maintenance costs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them are in local authority care and the council isn't paying anything towards any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! **You are eligible for Child Benefit for all three of your children: Alex, Blake, and Charlie.**

Here's a summary of your eligibility:

**Alex (age 3):** You are eligible because Alex is under 16, lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for their care.

**Blake (age 8):** You are eligible because although Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks (approximately 18 weeks), there is an important exception to the 12-week rule. Since Blake is in a UK hospital and you regularly spend money on him (such as for clothes, magazines, and snacks), you remain entitled to Child Benefit for Blake.

**Charlie (age 10):** You are eligible because Charlie is under 16, lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for their care.

Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for any of your children and the local council isn't paying towards their accommodation or maintenance, you meet all the requirements.

**Please note:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, but you can still claim the benefit.

I hope Blake recovers soon, and please don't hesitate to contact the Child Benefit Office if your circumstances change.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 3 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 8 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 10 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 18 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 3 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for them. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and the local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance. Alex meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

Blake is 8 years old (under 16) and has been in hospital for approximately 18 weeks, which exceeds the 12-week limit. However, according to the rules, an exception applies when a child is in a UK hospital and you regularly spend money on them. You have confirmed that you regularly spend money on Blake (buying clothes, magazines, snacks, etc.), which means you remain entitled to Child Benefit for Blake despite the hospital stay exceeding 12 weeks. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and the local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance.

Charlie is 10 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for them. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie, and the local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance. Charlie meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in Leeds, UK, and has three children: Alex (3), Blake (8), and Charlie (10) - all under 16 years old. Alex and Charlie live at home with the claimant. Blake has been in hospital for approximately 18 weeks, but the claimant regularly spends money on Blake's needs (clothes, magazines, snacks), which qualifies as an exception to the 12-week hospital rule. The claimant is responsible for all three children, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them, and the local council is not paying towards any child's accommodation or maintenance. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for all three children.
